note_data.title: Algorithm design and problem-solving
keyPoints: An **algorithm** is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem that must be finite, unambiguous, and have clearly defined inputs and outputs
**Pseudocode** uses structured English to represent algorithms without the syntax constraints of a programming language
**Flowcharts** use standardized symbols (oval for start/end, rectangle for process, diamond for decision) to visually represent algorithm flow
**Trace tables** track variable values through each step of algorithm execution to test correctness and identify logical errors
**Abstraction** involves removing unnecessary details and focusing only on essential features relevant to the problem being solved
**Decomposition** breaks complex problems into smaller, manageable sub-problems that can be solved independently
**Pattern recognition** identifies similarities between problems to apply existing solution methods
Algorithm design follows a systematic approach: understand problem, identify inputs/outputs, plan solution, implement, test and refine

definitions.definition: A finite sequence of unambiguous instructions that can be followed to solve a problem
A method of writing algorithms using structured English that is independent of programming language syntax
The process of hiding unnecessary implementation details while showing only essential features of an object or process
A technique used to test algorithms by tracking the values of variables at each step of execution
Breaking down a complex problem into smaller, more manageable sub-problems

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of the steps or process involved
Fill in missing parts of flowcharts, pseudocode or trace tables accurately

modelAnswer.question: Explain why trace tables are used when designing algorithms. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Trace tables are used to test the logic of algorithms by recording the values of variables at each step of execution. They help identify logical errors in the algorithm before implementation because any incorrect outputs can be spotted during the tracing process. Trace tables also help programmers understand how the algorithm works step-by-step, making debugging easier.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: To test algorithm logic/identify errors
Mark 2: Track/record variable values through execution
Mark 3: Help with debugging/understanding algorithm flow

commonMistakes: Never confuse pseudocode with actual programming code — pseudocode should be language-independent using structured English
Students often forget to include decision diamonds in flowcharts for selection statements
In trace tables, always show variable values after each step, not just final values
Don't mix flowchart symbols — use rectangles for processes and diamonds for decisions only
Abstraction is not just 'simplification' — it specifically means hiding unnecessary implementation details
examinerTips: Trace table questions appear frequently in Paper 2 - practice following algorithm steps methodically
When drawing flowcharts, always start and end with oval shapes
For pseudocode questions, use proper indentation and structured keywords like IF...THEN...ELSE
Algorithm design questions often worth 6-8 marks - plan your solution before writing
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Standard flowchart showing oval start/end symbols, rectangular process boxes, diamond decision symbols, and arrows indicating flow direction with proper labeling for Yes/No branches
